# **Refinement of FiTrack Biometric Tracking and Camera Positioning Specifications for Professional Kinematic Analysis**

The evolution of automated fitness tracking from recreational movement detection to academically rigorous biometric analysis requires a comprehensive synthesis of high-level sports science and advanced computer vision. To establish a professional-standard framework for the FiTrack system, it is necessary to integrate the physiological guidelines of the National Academy of Sports Medicine (NASM) and the National Strength and Conditioning Association (NSCA) with mathematical models that correct for the inherent limitations of monocular 2D pose estimation. Professional implementation demands a nuanced understanding of joint angle thresholds, geometric distortion correction, anthropometric normalization, and real-time state-transition logic. By standardizing these parameters, the system can transition from simple repetition counting to a diagnostic tool capable of identifying kinematic compensations and prescribing corrective exercise protocols.

## **Biomechanical Gold Standards and Joint Angle Thresholds in Elite Resistance Training**

Professional fitness tracking is predicated on the maintenance of structural integrity throughout a movement's range of motion (ROM). Elite sports science literature identifies five primary kinetic chain checkpoints—the ears, shoulders, hips, knees, and ankles—which must be monitored as a synchronized unit.1 The NASM Optimum Performance Training (OPT) model emphasizes that movement quality is the foundation of stabilization, strength, and power, necessitating specific joint angle thresholds for every phase of resistance.2

### **Kinematic Parameters and Parallelism in the Standard Squat**

The barbell or bodyweight squat is a multi-joint compound movement requiring the integration of the lumbo-pelvic-hip complex (LPHC) and the lower extremity.3 The eccentric phase is characterized by a triple-flexion pattern involving hip flexion, knee flexion, and ankle dorsiflexion.3 Professional standards define "gold standard" depth as the point where the thighs are parallel to the floor or slightly below, which typically corresponds to approximately ![][image1] of hip flexion and ![][image2] to ![][image3] of ankle dorsiflexion.3

A critical biometric for FiTrack implementation is the relationship between the trunk and the tibia. In an ideal squat pattern, the torso angle and the shin angle must remain parallel throughout the movement.3 Deviations in this parallel relationship provide diagnostic insights into muscle imbalances or skeletal limitations. For instance, a trunk-tibia angle greater than ![][image4] (trunk flexion exceeding tibia inclination) suggests a "hip-dominant" bias, often used to minimize loading on the knee joint in individuals with patellofemoral pain or post-ACL reconstruction.4 Conversely, a negative trunk-tibia angle indicates a "knee-dominant" bias, which increases the flexion moment at the knee and maximizes quadriceps recruitment but may exacerbate posterior shear forces.4

| Squat Parameter | Biometric Standard/Threshold | Biomechanical Significance |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Depth (Apex) | Thighs parallel to floor or lower | Maximizes quadriceps and gluteal hypertrophy 3 |
| Hip Flexion | ![][image5] | Necessary for full range of motion without lumbar rounding 3 |
| Ankle Dorsiflexion | ![][image6] | Prevents early heel lift and forward weight shift 3 |
| Trunk-Tibia Parallelism | Deviation ![][image7] | Maintains center of mass over the mid-foot 4 |
| Foot Rotation | ![][image8] outward | Normal anatomical positioning for pelvic alignment 3 |
| Knee Tracking | Alignment over 2nd/3rd toes | Prevents valgus stress and ACL injury risk 3 |

Advanced diagnostic tracking must also monitor for femoral version and acetabular alignment, which can influence an individual's "safe" squat depth.7 For example, individuals with femoral retroversion typically require an increased outward toe-rotation (beyond ![][image9]) to achieve adequate depth without hip impingement.3 Professional tracking systems should categorize these variations to distinguish between technical failure and anatomical preference.

### **Biometric Precision and Stability in the Standard Push-up**

The standard push-up is essentially a "moving plank," requiring the subject to maintain a rigid straight line from the ears through the shoulders, hips, and knees to the ankles.1 This alignment ensures that the LPHC is adequately stabilized by the core musculature, particularly the transverse abdominis and gluteus maximus.1 The primary failure biometric in push-ups is "hip sag," where the pelvis drops below the line of the torso, indicating underactive core stabilizers and increasing stress on the lumbar spine.1

Professional joint angle targets for push-up depth require the nose, chest, and belly button to reach the same height relative to the floor or wall.9 From a technical standpoint, the elbows should be positioned at approximately a ![][image10] angle relative to the torso, creating an "arrowhead" configuration rather than a "T-shape".11 This angle minimizes the risk of subacromial impingement and maximizes the mechanical advantage of the pectoralis major.11 The concentric phase must end at full arm extension, with particular attention to preventing scapular elevation or winging, which suggests underactivity in the serratus anterior and mid-to-lower trapezius.9

| Push-up Component | Kinetic Requirement | Biometric Angle/Target |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Setup Alignment | Straight line from ears to ankles | Moving plank/rigid unit 1 |
| Elbow Positioning | Arrow shape (![][image10] from torso) | Joint safety and pectoral engagement 11 |
| Standard Depth | Nose, chest, navel at same height | Full range of motion for chest recruitment 9 |
| Arm Extension | Full ROM at the elbow | Scapular stability and triceps engagement 9 |
| Stabilization | Prevent scapular winging/elevation | Maintain shoulder blade retraction 9 |

Tracking systems must also incorporate movement tempo as a biometric quality indicator. NASM recommends a 4/2/1 tempo scheme—4 seconds for the eccentric descent, a 2-second isometric "hover" in the bottom position for stability, and a 1-second explosive concentric push.9 Deviations from this tempo, such as "bouncing" out of the bottom position, provide critical data on the subject's rate of force production and joint control.

### **Kinematic Profiling and Cheat-Detection in the Biceps Curl**

The biceps curl, although a single-joint isolation movement, is frequently performed with compensations that reduce the effective load on the target musculature. The standard range of motion extends from ![][image11] (full extension) to ![][image12] (full flexion).13 The primary biometric for tracking is the suppression of "elbow drift," where the elbow joint moves anteriorly from the side of the body during the concentric phase, thereby engaging the anterior deltoids and shortening the biceps moment arm.14

Research into the neuromuscular activity of the biceps brachii partitions the curl cycle into three distinct phases: ![][image13] (initial lengthening), ![][image14] (peak torque), and ![][image15] (peak contraction).15 In movements like the incline dumbbell curl, the long head of the biceps is initially placed in a lengthened state, which results in higher activation throughout the entire ROM compared to the preacher curl, where the biceps are initially shortened and activation peaks early.15 Professional trackers should monitor the torso for "swing" or momentum, which is defined by horizontal displacement of the acromion joint during the lift.

| Biceps Curl Phase | Tracking Metric | Biometric Requirement |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Initiation | Elbow angle ![][image16] | Full extension and supination 13 |
| Concentric | Constant velocity (![][image17]) | Monitor for unintentional velocity loss 17 |
| Peak Contraction | Elbow angle ![][image5] | Full flexion without torso swing 13 |
| Eccentric | Controlled return (2-4 sec) | Maintain tension and shoulder retraction 18 |
| Compensation | Anterior elbow displacement | Prevent drift to anterior deltoid 14 |

Professional-grade velocity tracking is essential for the biceps curl, as the loss of velocity between repetitions is a highly reliable indicator of fatigue and relative load intensity.17 An unintentional drop in concentric velocity signifies that the subject is approaching their repetition maximum, allowing the system to provide real-time feedback on "reps in reserve" (RIR).

## **Geometric Constraints and Optical Correction in Monocular 2D Pose Estimation**

The transition of these biomechanical standards into a mobile tracking system is complicated by the geometric constraints of monocular vision. Mobile camera systems are subject to significant optical distortions that can misrepresent the spatial relationships between joints. Professional tracking must account for perspective foreshortening, lens distortion, and camera height ambiguity to ensure that joint angle calculations remain accurate across diverse environments.

### **Perspective Foreshortening and Depth Reconstruction**

Perspective distortion occurs when the distance from the subject to the camera significantly alters the relative scale of objects within the frame.20 In the context of pose estimation, this manifests as foreshortening: the apparent shortening of a limb when it is not parallel to the camera's imaging plane.20 For example, during a sagittal-view squat, if the subject's hips rotate slightly away from the camera, the detected length of the femur will decrease, leading to a false calculation of the knee joint angle.23

A promising approach to resolving this is Taylor’s geometric depth reconstruction, which exploits foreshortening information to estimate the missing ![][image18]\-dimension.23 This model assumes that the 2D pose is a result of scaled orthographic projection, where the depth displacement (![][image19]) between joint ![][image20] and joint ![][image21] is calculated as a function of the 3D world limb length (![][image22]) and the projected image length (![][image23]):

![][image24]  
where ![][image25] is the scaling factor.23 While this provides a mathematical foundation for 3D lifting, its reliability is challenged by the non-linear perspective of real-world mobile cameras.20 Professional implementation requires homography mapping to transform the distorted measurement plane back to a rectified imaging plane.21 This allows the system to measure angles as if the camera were positioned perfectly perpendicular to the subject.

### **Lens Distortion Models: Radial, Tangential, and Rational**

Smartphone lenses, particularly those optimized for wide-angle views, exhibit severe radial distortion.27 Radial distortion manifests as barrel distortion (outward bowing of parallel lines) or pincushion distortion (inward bowing), which causes linearity to decrease as distance from the optical axis increases.28 Tangential distortion occurs when the lens and the image sensor are not perfectly aligned, introducing asymmetric warping into the frame.27

To achieve sub-pixel precision in biometric tracking, systems must employ an integrated lens distortion model. The rational polynomial model is standard for modern hardware, utilizing six radial coefficients (![][image26]) and two tangential coefficients (![][image27]) 27:

![][image28]  
![][image29]  
where ![][image30] is the radius from the optical center.27 Experimental data indicates that local (regional) distortion correction, which estimates coefficients for individual quadrants of the image, can improve performance by ![][image31] over global techniques.27 This is particularly relevant for fitness tracking, where the subject often remains in a specific region of the frame, such as the center-bottom quadrant during a squat.

| Distortion Metric | Standard Correction Strategy | Technical Implementation |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Barrel/Pincushion | Rational Polynomial Model | CALIB\_RATIONAL\_MODEL (OpenCV) 27 |
| Tangential Warping | Misalignment Coefficients | Inclusion of ![][image32] parameters 27 |
| Keystone Distortion | Homography Matrix | Image plane rectification mapping 21 |
| Optical Center Offset | Center of Distortion (COD) Fit | Linear fit of parabolic coefficients 31 |
| Pixel Quantization | Sub-pixel Corner Detection | cornerSubPix refinement 27 |

### **Environmental Audit Protocol for Pose Estimation Accuracy**

The accuracy of pose estimation is fundamentally limited by the quality of the visual data. Lighting and contrast act as environmental filters; poor illumination introduces ISO noise and motion blur, which degrade the precision of landmark detection.32 Professional protocols mandate a minimum ambient illumination of ![][image33] lux, though "bright" conditions (![][image34] lux) are associated with the lowest mean absolute errors in joint angle measurement.34

Luminance contrast is the primary driver of successful tracking. High contrast between the subject and the background allows the model to distinguish distal joints (wrists, ankles) from the surrounding environment, which is critical since these joints consistently exhibit higher errors than proximal joints like the hips.35 Professional environmental audits should prioritize backlighting or ring lighting to create sharp silhouettes, minimizing shadows that can be misinterpreted as limb boundaries.37

| Environmental Factor | Target Threshold | Performance Impact |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Minimum Illumination | ![][image33] Lux | Reduces texture loss and detector jitter 34 |
| Average Luminance | ![][image35] cd/m² | Ensures visual comfort and frame clarity 40 |
| Frame Rate | ![][image36] FPS | Minimizes motion blur during high-velocity lifts 41 |
| Resolution | ![][image37]p (min) | Provides sub-pixel density for angle math 41 |
| Stability | Deviation ![][image38] | Essential for temporal consistency in Rep-counting 42 |

## **Advanced Normalization and Link-Length Ratio Implementation**

Scale invariance is a core requirement for a professional tracking system. Variations in distance from the camera and subject-specific anthropometry must be neutralized to allow for a standardized comparison of movement quality. This is achieved through the integration of link-length ratios and hierarchical feature extraction.

### **Winter’s Anthropometric Tables and Biometric Normalization**

The work of David A. Winter provides the foundational segmental ratios used in biomechanics to describe the "standard human".43 These ratios, derived from cadaveric and longitudinal studies, allow a tracking system to calculate the true anatomical length of occluded limbs by referencing the length of visible segments.44

| Segment | Length Ratio (% of Height) | Mass Ratio (% of Body Mass) |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Upper Arm | ![][image39] | ![][image40] |
| Forearm | ![][image41] | ![][image42] |
| Thigh | ![][image43] | ![][image44] |
| Lower Leg | ![][image45] | ![][image46] |
| Trunk | ![][image47] | ![][image48] |
| Foot | ![][image49] | ![][image50] |

Professional tracking logic utilizes these link-length ratios to perform a two-stage normalization:

1. **Skeleton Normalization:** detected 2D pixel coordinates are mapped onto a predefined standard 3D skeleton with fixed bone lengths.45 This disentangles the subject's unique body proportions from the movement itself.  
2. **Viewpoint Normalization:** using the root joint (the mid-hip) as a 3D origin, the system rotates the pose to a canonical orientation, allowing for consistent measurement of joint angles regardless of the camera's angle or height.45

### **Scale-Invariant Architectures: ScaleFormer and VTTransPose**

Modern pose estimation frameworks, such as ScaleFormer, utilize hybrid architectures to address target scale variation.46 By combining Swin Transformers for global structural hierarchical feature extraction with ConvNeXt for local detail enhancement, these systems maintain performance even when the subject is far from the lens.46 Advanced models like VTTransPose further refine this by implementing twin attention modules that capture global relationships between joints, reducing resource consumption while improving AP (Average Precision) by approximately ![][image51] on standard datasets.48

Scale consistency is particularly vital for detecting subtle compensations. For example, a ![][image52] error in keypoint localization on the ankle can lead to a ![][image2] error in knee flexion angle, potentially mislabeling a valid squat as shallow.35 Professional implementation must therefore employ multi-scale feature fusion and temporal filtering to sharpen heatmap distributions and refine distal joint coordinates.35

## **Real-Time Tracking Architecture: Finite State Machine and Velocity Triggers**

Repetition counting and form validation are governed by a real-time tracking architecture that segments continuous motion into discrete physiological states. Professional standards require an FSM logic that can handle noise, distinguish between sub-phases, and predict fatigue.

### **Finite State Machine (FSM) Logic and Hysteresis**

An exercise-specific FSM segments the video stream based on joint velocity and displacement.50 The machine operates through a series of transition triggers:

* **Trigger 1: Initial Position/Dormant.** The system confirms the five kinetic chain checkpoints. For a squat, this is the standing position with velocity ![][image53].  
* **Trigger 2: Eccentric Descent.** Triggered by a sustained negative vertical velocity (![][image54]). The system begins monitoring for common errors, such as knee valgus or trunk-tibia divergence.  
* **Trigger 3: Bottom/Apex Transition.** Entered when ![][image17] approaches zero and the joint angle reaches the target depth (e.g., hip flexion ![][image55]). Professional NASM standards recommend a 2-second isometric "hover" here to enhance joint stability.9  
* **Trigger 4: Concentric Ascent.** Triggered by positive vertical velocity. The system monitors for force production patterns and fatigue-induced velocity loss.  
* **Trigger 5: Repetition Reset.** Entered when the subject returns to the initial position.

To prevent false counting caused by sensor jitter or minor oscillations, the logic must implement hysteresis.50 This involves using dual thresholds for state transitions; for example, the eccentric phase may be triggered when the hip moves 10 pixels downward but only transitions to the concentric phase if it moves at least 20 pixels total, ensuring that micro-movements are rejected as noise.

### **Velocity-Based Training (VBT) and Minimal Velocity Thresholds**

Professional implementation integrates VBT metrics into the FSM to calculate intensity and daily 1RM readiness without the need for maximal testing.53 The association between mean concentric velocity (MCV) and relative load is linear, allowing the system to predict performance based on submaximal repetitions.17

| Exercise | Typical MVT (m/s) | Significance of Velocity Loss |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Back Squat | ![][image56] | Indicator of total muscular failure 53 |
| Bench Press | ![][image56] | Higher stability allows lower velocity floor 54 |
| Biceps Curl | ![][image56] | Small muscle group fatigue onset 17 |
| Snatch | ![][image56] | High power requirement prevents low-velocity success 53 |

A professional tracker must monitor "velocity loss" within a set. A ![][image57] drop in MCV from the fastest repetition is a standard biometric trigger to terminate a set, as further repetitions typically lead to a breakdown in postural integrity and a regression into faulty movement patterns.17 This "neural continuum" ensures that the athlete trains within their current physiological capacity while minimizing injury risk.1

## **Validation and Benchmarking via Authentic Gym Datasets**

The final stage of refining the FiTrack system is validation against gold-standard references. While current monocular models have mean absolute errors in the ![][image58] to ![][image59] range—above the ![][image60] threshold often required for clinical diagnosis—their reliability (ICC) is high, making them suitable for professional coaching assessments.35

The M3GYM dataset represents the professional standard for this validation.57 Unlike laboratory datasets, M3GYM captures 47 million frames of fitness activities—including Yoga, Pilates, and strength training—in an environment with floor mirrors and seven different lighting conditions.57 This multimodal data, which includes expert assessments of action quality for over 500 actions, provides the "ground truth" necessary to train the FiTrack FSM logic to handle real-world challenges such as mutual occlusion and reflection interference.58

## **Synthesis and Strategic Refinement Summary**

Professional refinement of FiTrack biometrics requires a multi-layered implementation strategy. The system must first calibrate for its hardware environment, utilizing rational polynomial models to correct lens distortion and homography mapping to rectify perspective. Biomechanical tracking must then be enforced through exercise-specific state machines that utilize Winter’s Link-Length Ratios for scale-invariant normalization. Form quality is judged against gold-standard joint angle thresholds—![][image1] hip flexion for squats, ![][image10] elbow-to-torso for push-ups, and ![][image12] ROM for curls—while velocity triggers provide an objective measure of fatigue and technical proficiency. By integrating these academically rigorous protocols, the system achieves a level of precision that transcends simple monitoring, providing a professional-standard diagnostic framework for human performance optimization.
